entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00055
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all read CME00055.

The XML's 642 L elements correspond to Furnivall's numbering through line 642; one separate Q element represents the unnumbered printed prayer line Pater noster / Aue /. Bryan's later edition numbers through 644 after treating that material differently.

access.accessMethod: Registered readers may submit the online collection item request form; manuscripts must be consulted in London and restrictions may apply
access.contact: British Library Reference Services; quote Add MS 89789
access.entityLabel: British Library Add MS 89789, The Red Book of Bath

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Arthur. The poem interrupts a Latin Brut at fols. 42v-46r in a composite civic compilation made for officials of Bath.

holding.notes: Purchased by the British Library from the Trustees of the Longleat House and Chattels Settlement in April 2025.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 89789, The Red Book of Bath
notes: The British Library catalogs two maps at fols. 70r-v, while its physical description says 69 folios and the IIIF sequence labels through fol. 69v; this minor foliation discrepancy remains unresolved.
Bath production and Somerset witness dialect are not treated as proof that the anonymous poem was composed in Bath.

physicalDescription.extent: iii modern paper endleaves + 69 manuscript folios; 1 volume
physicalDescription.format: Gothic cursive minuscule; Arthur in two columns
notes: Contemporary oak-board binding on five bands with a reddish-pink leather chemise and metal bosses.
A hinged cavity in the upper board formerly held scales and weights for measuring gold.
Arthur occupies fols. 42v-46r; the Latin Brut runs fols. 35v-42r and resumes at 46v-53v.

rights.basis: The manuscript and its anonymous poem date to the fifteenth century, and Arthur was published in 1864; no modern authorial copyright subsists in the medieval text itself.
rights.component: The medieval Arthur text and other medieval contents as intellectual works
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where the medieval works are out of copyright

entities.description: A 157-canvas color IIIF surrogate of the complete physical manuscript object, from front cover through all endleaves and manuscript leaves to the back cover, spine, fore-edge, head-edge, and tail-edge.
entities.label: British Library complete digital facsimile of Add MS 89789
notes: Arthur is a portion of this complete surrogate, at fols. 42v-46r, canvases 95-102; the cached object is deliberately not cropped to those eight canvases.

The downloader requested width 1800. The provider returned its full-profile derivative for canvases 1-155, generally about 1390 by 2000 pixels, while the head- and tail-edge images honored approximately 1800-pixel width.

bibliographic.citation: Mildred Willingham Bryan, A Critical Edition and Verse Translation of Arthur (PhD diss., University of Alabama, 1977).
bibliographic.title: A Critical Edition and Verse Translation of Arthur

dateStatements.label: dissertation date
dateStatements.notes: Some later bibliographies cite 1978; the dissertation title page and institutional record give 1977.

entities.label: Bryan 1977 critical edition and verse translation
notes: Includes introduction, critical text, explanatory notes, verse translation, glossary, index of proper names, and bibliography.

Bryan's critical text numbers the last line 644, whereas Furnivall/CME end at 642; the divergence begins where Bryan separately displays and counts Pater Noster and Ave material that Furnivall prints as one unnumbered prayer line.
This later edition was not a source for the nineteenth-century Furnivall edition or the U-M encoding.

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference or Rights Services to interpret RAQ ID 16.2/20170119 for republication in a public Git repository and print-on-demand research context.
Request a durable replacement for the manifest's currently broken image-reuse link.
Keep the whole-manuscript ZIP gitignored until the provider's UK carve-out is resolved.
notes: The manifest says Public Domain in most countries other than the UK and therefore does not support an unqualified worldwide-publication claim.
openQuestions.question: What exact reuse terms apply in the United Kingdom to the British Library Add MS 89789 IIIF images?

next_steps: Collate Project Gutenberg's first-edition text against the scanned 1869 second edition and the manuscript facsimile.
Separate revised introductory claims from changes to the Middle English transcription itself.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual readings changed between Furnivall's 1864 first edition and his 1869 second edition?

next_steps: Consult the complete Kooper-Marvin article and its diagnostic comparisons.
Distinguish a modern comparison witness such as Bodleian MS Wood empt. 8 from the unidentified historical exemplar used by the poet.
openQuestions.question: Can the precise textual branch or lost exemplar behind Arthur's Oldest-Version Anglo-Norman Prose Brut source be identified?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the maps are unnumbered, historically foliated 70, or cataloged by a superseded foliation.
Preserve current IIIF canvas labels unless the institution issues a correction.
notes: The discrepancy does not affect Arthur's securely labeled range at fols. 42v-46r.
openQuestions.question: How should the British Library catalog's references to fols. 70r-v be reconciled with its stated extent of 69 folios and IIIF labels ending at fol. 69v?

reviewNotes: Every access and rights statement is scoped to its named entity or component; medieval text, nineteenth-century edition, modern encoding, provider scan, and repository output are separate layers.

The complete publicly obtainable physical manuscript object is cached: covers, boards, endleaves, every manuscript leaf, spine, fore-edge, head-edge, and tail-edge. The work locator does not crop that bundle.
The British Library IIIF manifest's own labels establish Arthur at canvases 95-102 (fols. 42v-46r), with the surrounding Latin Brut retained at canvases 94 and 103 and throughout the complete bundle.
The Furnivall PDF is a complete scan of a composite bound volume; Arthur's edition component is mapped separately rather than treating adjacent EETS works as part of CME00055.
Current public access and rights findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-12.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item CME00055, keyed from Frederick J. Furnivall's 1869 second edition. That edition revises his 1864 transcription of the poem's sole witness, the Red Book of Bath, formerly Longleat House MS 55 and now British Library Add MS 89789. The complete 157-canvas manuscript surrogate is cached, with Arthur mapped inside it to fols. 42v-46r, canvases 95-102. Later critical editions and source studies are collateral scholarship, not hidden ancestors of the CME text.

catalogSummary.title: Arthur

catalogingSubject.label: Arthur
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse chronicle conventionally titled Arthur: a complete account of Arthur's life and reign preserved uniquely as an interpolation in the Red Book of Bath. The intellectual work is distinct from the surrounding Latin Brut, its sole manuscript witness, Furnivall's editions, later critical editions, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and all photographic reproductions.

contentParts.label: Arthur

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; 642 L elements plus one separately encoded, unnumbered Pater noster / Aue Q line

notes: No formal internal sections are encoded; narrative episodes in the summary are not promoted to manuscript divisions.
The Latin Brut resumes on fol. 46v and is outside this work boundary.
contentParts.summary: A continuous narrative from Arthur's parentage, coronation, Round Table, continental conquests and conflict with Rome through Mordred's betrayal, Arthur's last battle, death, and burial at Glastonbury, ending by directing readers to the French book for more.

assertion.notes: This is the commonly accepted probable range, not a dated authorial copy.

dateStatements.display: Uniquely attested in the Red Book of Bath, made c. 1412-1430
notes: The witness date is not silently converted into a composition date.

extent.description: Furnivall's source edition numbers the continuous poem through line 642, exactly matching the XML's 642 L elements. Between source-edition lines 632 and 633, the XML separately encodes the single unnumbered display 'Pater noster / Aue /' as Q; Bryan's later critical edition splits and counts this prayer material, so his ending is renumbered through 644. The XML also contains one post-poem editorial commentary paragraph outside the work. The poem occupies manuscript fols. 42v-46r and Furnivall printed pp. 1-19.

formStatements.description: A 642-line poem in Furnivall's source-edition numbering, predominantly in rhyming four-stress couplets, with occasional alternate-rhyme passages and metrical variation.

assertion.notes: Useful for discovery, but the work's chronicle form and Brut context make a single unqualified romance classification too narrow.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Some Latin belongs to manuscript marginal annotation or Furnivall's apparatus rather than the continuous Middle English verse.

scope.description: Brief prayers, Arthur's epitaph, closing formula, and manuscript marginalia

notes: The preferred title is conventional and editor-supplied; the manuscript is untitled.
Work composition, physical witness production, editorial publication, digital encoding, and photographic reproduction are kept as separate layers.
The full 157-canvas manuscript surrogate is bound in the lineage manifest, while this record maps only the poem's exact folio and canvas range.
The source claim is updated from Furnivall's broad Geoffrey-of-Monmouth account to Kooper and Marvin's evidence for the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut.

next_steps: Consult current dialect and textual scholarship without treating Bath manuscript production as proof of authorship there.
Compare linguistic strata against the now-public full manuscript images.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition date and place be narrowed beyond the probable 1350-1400 range and the South-Western language of its sole copy?

next_steps: Consult the complete Kooper-Marvin comparison and collate its diagnostic readings against the manuscript.
Do not equate a comparison codex with the lost historical exemplar without evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which copy or textual branch of the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut most closely represents the poem's immediate French source?

assertion.notes: A witness-language classification does not by itself prove the poet's exact place of composition.

scope.description: Language of the sole surviving copy

notes: Bath is the manuscript's production place, not a confirmed place of composition for the poem.

scope.description: Production of the sole manuscript witness

assertion.notes: No evidence securely identifies the poet, and the person who inserted or copied the poem into the Latin chronicle need not have composed it.

scope.description: The 1864/1869 source edition rather than authorship of the medieval work

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
The fall and death of King Arthur

scope.description: Mordred's betrayal, the final battle, Arthur's death, and Glastonbury burial
Narratorial invocations and the concluding Pater and Ave request

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse chronicle recounting Arthur's life from conception and coronation through the Round Table, conquests in Britain and France, war against the Roman emperor Lucius, and the return to Mordred's rebellion. Arthur defeats Mordred but is mortally wounded, dies at Glastonbury, and is buried there; the poem closes with prayer and directions to a French book and a continuation about later kings.

Arthur is interpolated between two portions of a Latin Brut in the Red Book of Bath. The surrounding chronicle and the manuscript's many civic, legal, medical, devotional, and historical texts are context for the witness but are not contents of CME00055.

assertion.notes: Bryan confirms that the poem is untitled in the manuscript and that Furnivall supplied this conventional title.
A current catalog description rather than a manuscript title.
Recorded as the closing formula, not treated as a title supplied by the manuscript.
